title: Case Studies in Nutrition & GI Health

quiz_questions.question_text: Iron-deficiency anemia is characterized by which type of red blood cells?

options.text: Normocytic, normochromic RBCs
Sickle-shaped RBCs
Microcytic, hypochromic RBCs (small, pale red blood cells)
Macrocytic, hyperchromic RBCs

quiz_questions.explanation: Iron deficiency leads to inadequate hemoglobin synthesis, producing red blood cells that are smaller than normal (microcytic) and paler than normal (hypochromic). This is the most common type of anemia worldwide, affecting over 1 billion people, particularly menstruating women, pregnant women, and young children.

quiz_questions.question_text: Low serum ferritin levels indicate:

options.text: High hemoglobin levels
Excessive iron stores in the body
Depleted iron stores, confirming iron deficiency
Normal iron metabolism

quiz_questions.explanation: Ferritin is the primary intracellular iron storage protein, and serum ferritin reflects total body iron stores. Low serum ferritin is the most specific laboratory marker for iron deficiency and is the first value to decrease as iron stores become depleted, even before anemia develops.

quiz_questions.question_text: Vitamin C enhances iron absorption by:

options.text: Increasing stomach acid production to dissolve iron supplements
Stimulating the liver to produce more transferrin
Reducing ferric iron (Fe3+) to ferrous iron (Fe2+), the form more readily absorbed by duodenal enterocytes
Blocking other minerals from competing with iron for absorption

quiz_questions.explanation: Vitamin C (ascorbic acid) acts as a reducing agent in the duodenal lumen, converting ferric iron (Fe3+) to ferrous iron (Fe2+). The ferrous form is recognized by the divalent metal transporter 1 (DMT1) on enterocytes, making it significantly more bioavailable. This is why iron supplements are often recommended to be taken with citrus juice.

quiz_questions.question_text: The most specific laboratory marker for diagnosing acute pancreatitis is:

options.text: White blood cell count
Serum amylase
Serum bilirubin
Serum lipase (elevated to 3 times or more above normal)

quiz_questions.explanation: Serum lipase is the preferred diagnostic marker for acute pancreatitis because it is more specific to the pancreas than amylase (which can also be elevated by salivary gland disease, bowel obstruction, and other conditions). Lipase remains elevated longer than amylase and a level three times the upper limit of normal is highly diagnostic.

quiz_questions.question_text: NPO (nil per os) status in acute pancreatitis is implemented to:

options.text: Allow time for antibiotics to work without food interference
Rest the pancreas by eliminating oral intake, which reduces stimulation of enzyme secretion
Prevent the patient from vomiting during treatment
Promote weight loss to reduce strain on the pancreas

quiz_questions.explanation: When food enters the duodenum, CCK stimulates the pancreas to secrete digestive enzymes. In acute pancreatitis, the pancreas is already undergoing autodigestion from prematurely activated enzymes, so NPO status reduces further stimulation of enzyme secretion, allowing the inflamed pancreas to rest and begin healing.

quiz_questions.question_text: The two most common causes of acute pancreatitis are:

options.text: Bacterial infection and autoimmune disease
Smoking and high-fat diet
Genetic mutations and viral hepatitis
Gallstones and alcohol abuse

quiz_questions.explanation: Gallstones and alcohol account for approximately 80% of acute pancreatitis cases. Gallstones can obstruct the common bile/pancreatic duct at the ampulla of Vater, causing backup and premature enzyme activation. Alcohol produces toxic metabolites (acetaldehyde) that directly damage acinar cells and promote premature zymogen activation.

quiz_questions.question_text: The first-line serological screening test for celiac disease is:

options.text: Fecal occult blood test
Anti-tissue transglutaminase (anti-tTG) IgA antibodies
Serum amylase levels
Serum ferritin levels

quiz_questions.explanation: Anti-tTG IgA antibodies are the most sensitive and specific serological marker for celiac disease, with sensitivity and specificity both exceeding 95%. Tissue transglutaminase is the autoantigen targeted by the immune system in celiac disease, and a positive result warrants confirmatory duodenal biopsy.

quiz_questions.question_text: The gold standard for confirming a diagnosis of celiac disease is:

options.text: An elimination diet trial for two weeks
Duodenal biopsy showing villous atrophy, crypt hyperplasia, and increased intraepithelial lymphocytes
A stool sample showing fat malabsorption
A positive blood antibody test alone

quiz_questions.explanation: While serological testing (anti-tTG antibodies) is used for screening, the definitive diagnosis of celiac disease requires upper endoscopy with multiple duodenal biopsies. The characteristic histological findings are villous atrophy (flattening of villi), crypt hyperplasia (elongated crypts), and increased intraepithelial lymphocytes (Marsh classification).

quiz_questions.question_text: Failure to thrive in a young child may indicate:

options.text: Malabsorption from conditions such as celiac disease, or inadequate nutritional intake
Dehydration from excessive fluid loss only
Normal developmental variation that requires no investigation
Only excessive caloric intake causing metabolic problems

quiz_questions.explanation: Failure to thrive refers to a child whose weight or rate of weight gain is significantly below that of other children of similar age and sex. In the GI context, malabsorptive disorders like celiac disease, cystic fibrosis, or food protein allergies can cause failure to thrive because the child cannot adequately absorb nutrients despite adequate caloric intake.

quiz_questions.question_text: Iron-rich dietary sources include:

options.text: Dairy products like milk, cheese, and yogurt
Red meat, spinach, lentils, beans, and fortified cereals
Citrus fruits and berries exclusively
Only iron supplements in pill form

quiz_questions.explanation: Dietary iron comes in two forms: heme iron (from animal sources like red meat, poultry, and fish), which is more readily absorbed (15-35% absorption rate), and non-heme iron (from plant sources like spinach, lentils, beans, and fortified cereals), which has a lower absorption rate (2-20%) but can be enhanced by vitamin C.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is ferritin?

options.text: The primary intracellular iron storage protein found mainly in the liver, spleen, and bone marrow, whose serum level reflects total body iron stores
The form of iron that circulates bound to hemoglobin in red blood cells
A transport protein in the blood that carries iron to the bone marrow
An enzyme that converts iron from ferrous to ferric form for absorption

quiz_questions.explanation: Ferritin is a large spherical protein complex that can store up to 4,500 iron atoms in its core in a non-toxic, bioavailable form. It is found primarily in hepatocytes, reticuloendothelial cells of the spleen, and bone marrow macrophages. Serum ferritin is the most useful clinical marker of total body iron stores, with low levels being the earliest indicator of iron depletion.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is refeeding syndrome?

options.text: Weight gain that occurs after a period of dieting
An allergic reaction to tube feeding formula
Nausea and vomiting that occurs when eating after a short fast

A potentially fatal condition occurring when a severely malnourished person is fed too rapidly, causing dangerous shifts in electrolytes (especially phosphate, potassium, and magnesium) as cells suddenly switch from fat to carbohydrate metabolism

quiz_questions.explanation: Refeeding syndrome occurs when carbohydrate intake is rapidly reintroduced after prolonged starvation. Insulin surges drive glucose, phosphate, potassium, and magnesium into cells, causing severe hypophosphatemia, hypokalemia, and hypomagnesemia. These electrolyte shifts can cause cardiac arrhythmias, respiratory failure, seizures, and death. Prevention requires gradual caloric reintroduction with close electrolyte monitoring.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is total parenteral nutrition (TPN)?

options.text: A specialized diet consisting only of easily digestible foods
Complete intravenous nutrition (glucose, amino acids, lipids, vitamins, minerals) delivered through a central venous catheter, bypassing the GI tract entirely
Liquid nutrition delivered through a nasogastric tube directly into the stomach
A high-calorie oral supplement given to malnourished patients between meals

quiz_questions.explanation: TPN provides all necessary macronutrients (dextrose for calories, amino acids for protein, and lipid emulsions for essential fatty acids and additional calories) plus micronutrients (vitamins, minerals, electrolytes) directly into the bloodstream via a central venous catheter. It is reserved for patients who cannot use their GI tract, such as those with bowel obstruction, severe pancreatitis, or short bowel syndrome.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'megaloblastic anemia' mean?

options.text: A type of anemia characterized by abnormally large, immature red blood cells (megaloblasts) in the bone marrow, typically caused by vitamin B12 or folate deficiency that impairs DNA synthesis
Anemia caused by iron deficiency producing small, pale red blood cells
Anemia caused by excessive red blood cell destruction in the spleen
A genetic condition producing abnormally shaped red blood cells

quiz_questions.explanation: Megaloblastic anemia occurs when B12 or folate deficiency impairs DNA synthesis in rapidly dividing cells, particularly red blood cell precursors in the bone marrow. The cells cannot divide properly and become abnormally large (megaloblasts/macrocytes). B12 deficiency also causes neurological damage (subacute combined degeneration of the spinal cord), distinguishing it from folate deficiency.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is prealbumin used for in clinical nutrition assessment?

options.text: It is a sensitive short-term marker of nutritional status and protein synthesis, with a half-life of approximately 2 days, allowing rapid detection of nutritional improvement or decline
It indicates kidney function and fluid balance
It measures the amount of iron stored in the body
It is a marker for liver disease severity

quiz_questions.explanation: Prealbumin (transthyretin) has a short half-life of approximately 2 days, compared to albumin's 20-day half-life, making it a much more sensitive real-time indicator of current nutritional status and response to nutritional intervention. It is commonly used in hospitalized patients to monitor whether nutritional support is adequate.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does the term 'villous atrophy' mean?

options.text: Overgrowth and enlargement of intestinal villi leading to excessive absorption
The flattening, shortening, or destruction of intestinal villi, resulting in dramatically reduced absorptive surface area and malabsorption
Normal aging of the intestinal lining with no clinical significance
Inflammation of the villi caused by bacterial infection

quiz_questions.explanation: Villous atrophy refers to the pathological flattening or loss of the normal finger-like projections (villi) that line the small intestine. This reduces the absorptive surface area and impairs nutrient uptake. It is the hallmark histological finding in celiac disease but can also occur in tropical sprue, autoimmune enteropathy, and certain infections.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a probiotic?

options.text: A vitamin supplement that enhances immune function
An antibiotic that specifically targets harmful gut bacteria
A live microorganism that, when administered in adequate amounts, confers a health benefit to the host by supporting the balance of beneficial gut flora
A dietary fiber supplement that feeds gut bacteria

quiz_questions.explanation: Probiotics are defined by the World Health Organization as live microorganisms that, when administered in adequate amounts, confer a health benefit on the host. Common probiotic strains include Lactobacillus and Bifidobacterium species. Evidence supports their use in preventing antibiotic-associated diarrhea, treating C. difficile recurrence, and managing some cases of IBS.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'enteral nutrition' mean?

options.text: A special oral diet consisting of pureed foods only
Nutrition delivered intravenously, bypassing the digestive system entirely
Nutrition absorbed through the skin via transdermal patches
Nutrition delivered directly into the gastrointestinal tract through a tube (nasogastric, nasoenteric, or gastrostomy tube) when a patient cannot eat by mouth but has a functioning GI tract

quiz_questions.explanation: Enteral nutrition involves delivering a liquid formula containing macronutrients, vitamins, and minerals directly into the GI tract via a tube. It is preferred over parenteral nutrition (IV) whenever the gut is functional because it maintains gut mucosal integrity, supports the gut microbiome, reduces infection risk, and is less expensive. Routes include nasogastric, nasojejunal, percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy (PEG), or jejunostomy tubes.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the Mediterranean diet?

options.text: A calorie-restricted diet designed specifically for weight loss
A strict low-carbohydrate, high-protein diet that eliminates all grains
A vegetarian diet that excludes all animal products
A dietary pattern emphasizing fruits, vegetables, whole grains, legumes, fish, olive oil, and moderate wine consumption, associated with reduced cardiovascular and GI disease risk

quiz_questions.explanation: The Mediterranean diet is a dietary pattern based on the traditional cuisines of countries bordering the Mediterranean Sea. It is rich in plant-based foods, healthy fats (especially olive oil), fish, and moderate wine. Extensive research has demonstrated its benefits in reducing cardiovascular disease, certain cancers, type 2 diabetes, and inflammatory conditions including GI disorders.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'nutritional assessment' mean in clinical practice?

options.text: Counting the number of calories a patient consumes daily
Simply weighing a patient and calculating their BMI

A comprehensive evaluation including dietary history, anthropometric measurements, physical examination, and laboratory tests (CBC, ferritin, B12, folate, vitamin D, albumin, prealbumin) to identify nutritional deficiencies and guide treatment
Ordering a single blood test to check cholesterol levels

quiz_questions.explanation: Clinical nutritional assessment is a systematic approach that combines subjective data (dietary history, appetite changes, GI symptoms) with objective data (weight changes, anthropometric measurements, physical signs of deficiency, and comprehensive laboratory tests). This multi-faceted approach enables identification of specific nutritional deficiencies and guides targeted interventions.

quiz_questions.question_text: A 30-year-old vegetarian woman presents with fatigue, pallor, and shortness of breath on exertion. Her labs show hemoglobin of 9 g/dL, MCV of 68 fL (low), and ferritin of 5 ng/mL (low). Her physician prescribes oral iron supplements and advises her to take them with orange juice. Why are these specific recommendations appropriate?

options.text: The anemia is caused by folate deficiency common in vegetarians, and iron with OJ treats both
The low MCV (microcytic) and low ferritin confirm iron-deficiency anemia; orange juice provides vitamin C, which converts Fe3+ to Fe2+ and enhances iron absorption in the duodenum
The low MCV and ferritin confirm B12 deficiency, and orange juice provides B12
Orange juice masks the unpleasant taste of iron supplements, improving compliance

quiz_questions.explanation: This patient has classic iron-deficiency anemia: low hemoglobin, low MCV (microcytic), and low ferritin (depleted iron stores). Vegetarians are at higher risk because plant-based (non-heme) iron is less bioavailable than heme iron from meat. Vitamin C in orange juice reduces ferric iron to ferrous iron, significantly enhancing duodenal absorption through the DMT1 transporter.

quiz_questions.question_text: A 50-year-old chronic alcoholic is admitted with severe epigastric pain radiating to the back. His serum lipase is 1500 U/L (normal < 60). He is placed NPO with IV fluids and pain management. Three days later, he is started on a clear liquid diet and slowly advanced. Why was the initial NPO period essential for his recovery?

options.text: NPO was required to allow antibiotics to fully penetrate the pancreatic tissue
NPO was needed to prevent him from consuming more alcohol while hospitalized
The patient's esophagus was inflamed and needed time to heal before eating
Withholding oral intake eliminates the CCK-mediated stimulation of pancreatic enzyme secretion, allowing the inflamed pancreas to rest and reducing the cycle of autodigestion

quiz_questions.explanation: In acute pancreatitis, the pancreas is undergoing autodigestion by prematurely activated enzymes. Any food entering the duodenum triggers CCK release, which stimulates the pancreas to secrete more enzymes, worsening the inflammation. NPO status breaks this cycle by eliminating the luminal stimulus for enzyme secretion, giving the pancreas time to recover. IV fluids maintain hydration during this period.

quiz_questions.question_text: A 3-year-old child is brought to the pediatrician for poor weight gain, chronic diarrhea, and abdominal bloating that began after introduction of solid foods. Blood work reveals anti-tTG antibodies are strongly positive. The mother asks if her child can ever eat bread again. What is the most accurate response?

options.text: Yes, the child will likely outgrow celiac disease by puberty and can resume eating wheat
The child should take medication to suppress the immune response and can continue eating normally
The child must follow a strict lifelong gluten-free diet (avoiding wheat, barley, and rye), but can eat bread made from gluten-free grains like rice flour or corn flour
The child must avoid all grains permanently, including rice and corn

quiz_questions.explanation: Celiac disease is a lifelong autoimmune condition that does not resolve with age. The only effective treatment is strict avoidance of gluten (found in wheat, barley, and rye). However, many grains are naturally gluten-free (rice, corn, quinoa, millet, buckwheat), and gluten-free breads and products made from these alternatives are widely available, allowing the child to enjoy varied meals.

quiz_questions.question_text: An elderly patient who has been hospitalized in the ICU for three weeks on mechanical ventilation is being transitioned from TPN to enteral (tube) feeding. The dietitian recommends starting with a low-calorie formula and advancing slowly over several days. Why is this gradual approach necessary?

options.text: Enteral formulas are too concentrated and must be diluted to prevent osmotic diarrhea
The patient's stomach has shrunk and cannot hold normal volumes of food
Rapid reintroduction of enteral nutrition can cause refeeding syndrome with dangerous drops in phosphate, potassium, and magnesium that can lead to cardiac arrhythmias and death
The patient needs time to regain the ability to chew and swallow food

quiz_questions.explanation: After prolonged malnutrition or fasting, the body adapts by depleting intracellular stores of phosphate, potassium, and magnesium. When carbohydrates are suddenly reintroduced, insulin secretion drives these electrolytes into cells, causing precipitous drops in serum levels. Severe hypophosphatemia can cause respiratory failure and cardiac arrest, which is why careful, gradual refeeding with electrolyte monitoring is essential.

quiz_questions.question_text: A patient with Crohn's disease affecting the terminal ileum has undergone multiple surgical resections, leaving her with only 100 cm of small bowel. She develops severe diarrhea, weight loss, and multiple vitamin deficiencies. The gastroenterologist starts her on TPN. What is the underlying condition, and why is TPN necessary?

options.text: She has short bowel syndrome with insufficient remaining intestinal length for adequate nutrient and fluid absorption, making TPN necessary to prevent malnutrition and dehydration
She has IBS, and TPN calms the overactive gut
She has celiac disease, and TPN provides gluten-free nutrition
She has lactose intolerance, and TPN avoids dairy-based nutrients

quiz_questions.explanation: Short bowel syndrome occurs when the remaining small intestinal length (normally 300-600 cm) is insufficient for adequate nutrient and fluid absorption. With only 100 cm remaining, this patient cannot absorb enough calories, fluids, and micronutrients through her GI tract alone. TPN provides complete nutrition intravenously, and intestinal adaptation may gradually reduce TPN dependence over months to years.

quiz_questions.question_text: A gastroenterologist is counseling a patient with recurrent C. difficile infection about fecal microbiota transplantation (FMT). The patient is skeptical. The doctor explains that FMT works by restoring the normal gut microbiome. What is the scientific rationale behind this treatment?

options.text: FMT provides immune cells from the donor that attack C. difficile directly
FMT introduces antibiotics directly into the colon to kill C. difficile
FMT changes the pH of the colon to make it inhospitable for C. difficile
FMT introduces a healthy donor's diverse gut bacteria into the patient's colon, restoring colonization resistance that prevents C. difficile from competing for nutrients and colonization sites

quiz_questions.explanation: FMT restores the diverse microbial ecosystem of the colon by transplanting stool from a healthy donor. The reestablished normal flora competes with C. difficile for nutrients and colonization sites, produces antimicrobial substances, and restores the bile acid metabolism that inhibits C. difficile germination. FMT has a cure rate exceeding 90% for recurrent CDI, far superior to antibiotics alone.

quiz_questions.question_text: A physician is evaluating a malnourished hospitalized patient and orders both albumin and prealbumin levels. Albumin is low at 2.5 g/dL (normal 3.5-5.0) and prealbumin is very low at 5 mg/dL (normal 20-40). After five days of adequate nutritional support, the physician rechecks both markers. Which will show improvement first, and why?

options.text: Albumin, because it is the more abundant protein and responds faster to nutrition
Both will improve at the same rate since they are both synthesized by the liver
Neither will improve in only five days; both require several weeks to respond
Prealbumin, because its shorter half-life (approximately 2 days) makes it a more sensitive and rapid indicator of nutritional response compared to albumin's 20-day half-life

quiz_questions.explanation: Prealbumin (transthyretin) has a half-life of approximately 2 days, so it responds rapidly to changes in nutritional status and should show measurable improvement within 3-5 days of adequate nutrition. Albumin has a much longer half-life of approximately 20 days, meaning it takes weeks to reflect nutritional improvement. This makes prealbumin the preferred marker for short-term nutritional monitoring in hospitalized patients.

quiz_questions.question_text: A 60-year-old woman with celiac disease who has been non-compliant with her gluten-free diet develops osteoporosis and is found to have very low vitamin D and calcium levels. Her physician explains that her GI condition directly contributed to her bone disease. What is the pathophysiological connection?

options.text: Ongoing villous atrophy from uncontrolled celiac disease impairs absorption of calcium and fat-soluble vitamin D in the small intestine, leading to deficiency and reduced bone mineralization
The gluten-free diet itself causes calcium deficiency because it eliminates dairy
Celiac disease causes the kidneys to excrete excess calcium
Gluten directly destroys bone tissue, causing osteoporosis

quiz_questions.explanation: In uncontrolled celiac disease, persistent villous atrophy in the duodenum and jejunum impairs calcium absorption (which requires active vitamin D and intact enterocytes). Fat malabsorption also impairs fat-soluble vitamin D absorption. Without adequate calcium and vitamin D, bone mineralization is compromised, leading to osteomalacia and accelerated osteoporosis. Strict adherence to a gluten-free diet allows villous recovery and improved absorption.

quiz_questions.question_text: A clinical dietitian is counseling a patient newly diagnosed with iron-deficiency anemia who is also a regular tea drinker. The dietitian advises the patient to avoid drinking tea with meals and instead take iron supplements on an empty stomach with vitamin C. What is the scientific basis for this dietary guidance?

options.text: Tea causes stomach acid to become too alkaline for iron absorption
Tannins and polyphenols in tea bind to non-heme iron and form insoluble complexes that cannot be absorbed, while vitamin C enhances absorption by reducing Fe3+ to the more absorbable Fe2+ form
Tea destroys the DMT1 transporters on enterocytes
Tea stimulates the liver to produce hepcidin, which blocks all iron absorption

quiz_questions.explanation: Tannins and polyphenols in tea (and coffee) are potent inhibitors of non-heme iron absorption because they form insoluble iron-tannate complexes in the gut lumen. Taking iron supplements between meals (when inhibitors are absent) and with vitamin C (which reduces Fe3+ to the more absorbable Fe2+ form) maximizes iron bioavailability and can significantly improve anemia correction.

quiz_questions.question_text: A medical team is debating whether to use enteral nutrition (tube feeding) or total parenteral nutrition (TPN) for a patient who had emergency abdominal surgery but whose small bowel is functioning. The senior physician states: 'If the gut works, use it.' What evidence-based reasons support this principle?

options.text: Enteral nutrition maintains gut mucosal integrity, supports the gut microbiome and immune function, reduces infectious complications (including catheter-related bloodstream infections), and preserves the intestinal barrier that prevents bacterial translocation
TPN is always preferable after surgery because the gut needs complete rest to heal
There is no evidence-based difference between enteral and parenteral nutrition
Enteral nutrition is only preferred because it is cheaper than TPN

quiz_questions.explanation: The principle 'if the gut works, use it' is supported by extensive evidence showing that enteral nutrition, compared to TPN, maintains the structural and functional integrity of the intestinal mucosa, prevents villous atrophy, supports the gut microbiome, stimulates gut-associated lymphoid tissue (GALT) immune function, reduces bacterial translocation across a compromised gut barrier, and lowers the risk of central line-associated bloodstream infections (CLABSIs) inherent to TPN.
